sentences.text: Yeah, I was talking about my arrest by the Gestapo.
And suddenly, someone came from an upper level, or a different office.
And I overheard, briefly, that-- words like, in German, like, we can't keep him.

With that, the stormtrooper who had threatened me the most, who told me he was going to hang me by my testicles on the flagpole outside, and all what went on, with the-- with the things they threatened me with, you wouldn't believe, besides deportation.
He said, we're going to let you go.
We're going to give you two minutes to leave this building.
And if you're not out of here and out of sight within two minutes, we will shoot you for trying to escape.
With that, he opened the door, and those big steel doors, which they had at headquarters, the Gestapo headquarters.
And I ran down the steps.
I see these steps in front of my eyes today.
I didn't walk those steps, I raced those steps down.
And I must have raced home like a gazelle.
And when I walked into the house, my stepmother said, what happened to you?
And I said to her, I became ill.
I did not tell her what happened.
There was so much on their minds and so much going on, I didn't want to burden them with my arrest.
And I knew, the most important thing, I had a signed document which was given to me at the social security office, which said that my social security had been paid.
I had an-- a document from the [GERMAN], the tax office, that my taxes were paid.
And I knew, with these documents, I would get the visa, and I would leave.
And I did leave then, Germany, within a day or two after all this happened.
In November of 1938, I got on the-- what must have been one of the very last children's transport to Holland.
And that's how I got out.
Yes.
I got out-- first of all, I-- I had a temperature of-- equivalent of 103, 104, after this beating.
And my mother, you know, really thought that I had become ill, but it must have been all this was a reaction to what happened to me.
And I-- I was in bed for two or three days.
And I was really more out of it than in like someone who had a-- a severe flu symptom and a very high temperature.
And then, when I became-- I got well again, is shortly thereafter, a few days thereafter, I left Germany.
That was it, to-- for Holland.
No.
I-- I-- Oh, yes.
I do know that it was sponsored, probably, by American funding, either something like the HIAS, or JUF, or whatever organizations were called in those days.
They were behind funding the transports.
Now, certainly, no one in Germany was able to award or could do this.
Mm-hm.
It's very easy.

You've got a rabble-rouser who gets up on a soapbox-- and I don't care if it's Hitler or it's a [NON-ENGLISH], someone who lives in this day and age, or these type of people, and they rouse the passion of the people and they rile them up.
You have a power.
These people have certain power over people, and they'll do almost anything to follow these type of leaders.
They will do almost anything.
Some of them do it because for economic reasons.
They think they can better themselves, if they beat up on other people.
And some of-- of-- religious fanatics, and some are-- the church, certainly, in-- in-- in Germany, was anti-Semitic.
Absolutely.
Catholic, Lutheran.
Martin Luther is one who preached anti-Semitism.
And the Catholic Church had it in their New Testament.
I mean, let's face it.
Anti-Semitism was very much promoted by the churches.
And that's why, if you ask me, how could it come about?
It was easy.
The people knew-- were anti-Jewish from the Middle Ages on down.
Absolutely.
It was very easy to bring that about.
When you, yourself, listened to Hitler, to a Hitler speech--
I did, I saw him in person.
Yes, absolutely.
It was because the people surrounding him-- I once was caught in a whole bunch of people in the city of Essen.
And I found out the reason they were all milling about, they were waiting-- awaiting Hitler's arrival.
And I was just about caught in the crowd.
And within a few minutes there, he came standing up in-- in a convertible, raised his hands in a German salute, which I did not.
And-- and suddenly, somebody sat next to me, pushed me.
He said that I would have to salute, which I did out of fear, actually.
And there was a charisma you can't believe.
The people were just like an angel from heaven came down the street.
And they were all that way.
They were all that way.
Absolutely.
Mm-hm.
Even though the Essen-Gelsenkirchen, the-- the communists, the socialists, the--
The-- the-- the people who were Communists and Social Democrats, they fell into two categories.
There were those that would follow movements.
There were many Communists and Social Democrats who suddenly became Nazis.

And then, there were those that were in the higher hierarchies of the Social Democrats, the Catholic party, and what have you, certainly, the Communist party, who was-- who were arrested, who were arrested by-- by the Nazis.
And I am sure-- I assure you that, in the beginning, Dachau was a camp for political prisoners, as much or more so than it was for the Jews.
They were all in the camps.
And many of them died and got killed there.
And among them, there was some-- some religious leaders-- some religious leaders.
And so that's what I said, some of them became Nazis, and others were deported by the Nazis.
Mm-hm.
I was immediately sent to a camp outside of Amsterdam first.
But that was only for about a week or so.
It was sort of like a transit camp.
And then, they sent us to youth hostels.
One, if I'm not mistaken, was near the city of Utrecht.
And the other was near the city of Deventer.
And there, we lived a very comfortable life.
We could work in the fields.
They had strawberries and other vegetables out in the fields.
We worked in the fields.
Some of us worked in carpenter shops that were in these youth hostels.
We worked, but we did the kind of work that would not interfere [CLEARS THROAT] with the economy of Holland.
We could not get a job, so to speak, and-- and work officially in Holland.
We would have to work within the youth hostels.
My parents, they were waiting very, very patiently, from that moment on, until the end of 1939, for their visa, for their number to come through at the German consulate in Stuttgart, Germany.
And towards the end of 1939, they finally called them.
They gave them their visa and my visa.
Even so, I was no longer living in Germany, but I got my visa at the same time my parents got theirs.
They went to Rotterdam, I went to Rotterdam.
We boarded a Dutch liner in December of 1939.
The war had already broken out in Germany.
It broke out in September of 1939.
And the three of us came-- came across the-- the sea.
No.
My dad was never arrested, except for the time he was actually beaten up and in the hospital.
That's the only time.
By way of New York, we came to Chicago.
Yes.
Mm-hm.
Well, they had-- they-- they got-- they-- they had gotten a small apartment, a furnished apartment in the South Shore area of Chicago.
It was a very nice little apartment.
And the three of us lived there rather comfortably, until a year or two later when we moved into a slightly larger apartment.
Well, I must say, to the credit of my parents, especially my stepmother, she wanted me to return to the gymnasium here, and she wanted me to go to college.
She absolutely did.
She said, now, you don't have to continue as a tailor.
You can go and resume your academic studies.
Because that's what, after all, was their goal for me way back in Germany.
And I said, no.
I said, you all have to work very hard, and there's very little money.
And I want to work.
And as long as I learn this trade, I'm going to get a job as a tailor.
And that's how it happened.
I mean--
My brother was here.
And then, of course, he moved in with us when we came here.
And he was younger.
And he went to high school here in the United States.
Mm-hm.
Not at all.
I had-- in my younger years, like when-- I mean, I-- I-- I had a knack for languages.
And I learned English already in high school, along with French and Latin.
And I-- I decided immediately to go to a night school here.
I attended a night school in Chicago, at Englewood High School, where I took two courses.
One was in English, and the other one was in American History.
I'm sort of a history buff anyway.
I like history, and so I enjoyed taking those two courses.
And I learned how to speak English, I-- I hope rather fluently this day.
And I learned a bit about American history.
And-- and all in all, I didn't find the transition difficult.
And one of the reasons was probably because I was only 18 years of age at that point, when I arrived here.
I was 17 when I left Germany.
And it's much-- it was much more difficult for older people.
Absolutely.
For my parents, much more difficult.
But I, myself, didn't find it difficult at all.
I-- I assimilated almost at once.
Yeah, he was a Fuller brush man.
That was the first job he had.
My aunt, in the meantime, was working in the flower store.
And my mother, also, then started to work in the same flower store.
And later on, after several years, the-- the florist that owned the store, he wanted to sell out.
And between my dad and my aunt, they got enough money together to buy out the florist.
And they had a flower shop here in South Shore for many, many years.
My father had the foresight, I must say, unlike other people, to send some money to foreign banks prior to the changing of the laws, prior to his leaving Germany.
So there was some money here, in the United States.
Not very much, but enough that he wouldn't become a burden to either the relatives or the government.
There was enough there.
But it was hard work from day one they arrived.
And they've really built up a nice nest egg, so they could live moderately in the United States.
Yes.
Almost from day one.
The moment we entered the war against Germany, I think, almost all of us young immigrants from Germany had the same desire.
Somehow, if we could only go back there to fight these sons of a bitches, OK?
That was our feeling.
And it didn't take very long thereafter, when the draft laws came about in early 1942.
And on the June 30th, 1942, I went into the United States Army.
Oh, yeah, they-- almost-- almost all the fellows I knew went into the service.
There were a few of them who got physical deferments, physical-- had physical disability, but very few.
Well, let me tell you.
I lived in South Shore, part of the city of Chicago.
Very few German-Jews lived there.
Most of them lived in the neighborhood of Hyde Park, which was just 10 minutes from South Shore.
But almost all the friends that I had and made, including my future wife, they all lived in Hyde Park.
And I spent more-- more time, social time, certainly, in Hyde Park than I did in South Shore.
On the 4th of July, 1942. '42?
Let me see that I didn't-- is that correct?
Or was it '41?
No, It was 1941.
I'm sorry.
On the 4th of July, 1941, we had our first official date.
I had met her the week before.
And at-- there was a club called the Jewish Center, where many of the young immigrants gathered, sort of a companionship.
And I met her there.
And there was a jukebox.
And we danced to the jukebox.
And I asked her for a date for the 4th of July.
And she made the sandwiches, and I probably brought the soda pop.
And our first date was in the neighborhood here, and at the 57th street beach in Hyde Park.
It was 1941.
That's correct, 4th of July.
And my wife, Elsa, came from Germany.
She was only, I believe, eight or nine years old when she came.
I-- I think it was in 1937 when she came over.
Her parents sent her all alone, as a little girl.
They realized that they could not hold on to the girls.
They-- they-- my father-in-law always thought that he could not get out, because he was hard of hearing.
And the American immigration laws, if you had a disability of some sort, they would-- they would not give you a visa.
But they were fortunate enough to get a visa eventually.
But they sent their daughter, all alone.
She lived here with relatives.
Yes.
My basic training was in Fort Sill, Oklahoma, in the artillery.
Yes, that's correct.

They sent me as a gunnery instructor to, what was then, Camp Hood, Texas, to the officer's candidate school, where we were to become instructors, or possibly even candidates, at the OCS school in Camp Hood, Texas.
That's correct.
The only-- never.
Really, never.
The only anti-Semitism I experienced was complete and total ignorance on the part of some Gentile soldiers who had never even seen a Jew, who-- who didn't even know what a Jew was like.
I always tell this story, because it's so typical of that.
I befriended a Sergeant from Oklahoma, a really nice fellow.
And he told me to be sure to visit me after the war.
And one day, we were playing some poker.
He said, boy, I'm glad when I get back home, because all these niggers.
You know, we have a sign as we come-- when you come into our town that says, 'No Jews or niggers allowed.' He came from a small town in Oklahoma.
And I said, Tommy, what are you talking about?
Just the other day, you said you want me to visit you when you are back home.
He said, well, of course I did.
I said, well, now you're telling us-- you're telling us that, in your town, you have a sign on the outside of town says, 'No Jews or niggers allowed.' But I'm a Jew.
Oh, come on.
You ain't no Jew.
I said, Tommy, I'm Jewish.
You ain't no Jew!
So this shows you the stereotyping amongst even our American people.
And this is the kind of ignorance-- anti-Semitism.
That's the only time I ever found that in the service.
No-- I have no other incidents whatsoever.
As a matter of fact, most of the friends I made in the Army, a vast majority of them were Gentile boys.
Do you think that same kind of ignorance also existed in Germany?
No.
In-- definitely not.
I think the German people were much more anti-Semitic.
Not that we don't have our shares in the United States, believe me, to this very day, and always have, but it was much more prevalent in Germany.
But it was-- it was not out in the open as much, until Hitler came to power.
It was sort of under the surface.
As I said, the churches, the teachings, all of this, it was always there, anti-Semitism.
And hey, they even had a Jewish chancellor after World War I, for a short period of time.
I think he was assassinated-- Rathenau.
He was Jewish.
So there-- there was the low-key anti-Semitism.
Yes.
Well, as it was, we were-- there were 30 of us sent down there.
We were to instruct the officer's candidates.
The problem was the school had hardly opened.
There were as many officer's-- officer's candidates as there were instructors.
And we really weren't doing very much.
We were just laying around our bunk, and so on.
And then, finally, one of my buddies, he got a rating.
He became a corporal.
And he was sitting there, and he was sewing the chevrons on his shirt and his jacket.
And I observed what he was doing, and it was awful.
He had no idea how to sew on chevrons.
Of course, he wasn't a tailor.
And I said, give me this-- your shirt and jacket.
And I took this from him, and I sewed on the chevrons.
And within no time at all, word got around that I was a tailor.
And-- and everyone came.
You know, I got this jacket issue, and the sleeves are too long.
The pants are too short.
And everyone had something for me to sew.
So I was sitting outside a so-called day room.
I put a little table out there, and I began to sew, just so I had something to do, because-- until we got permanently assigned to our job duties.
And I was sewing when the company commander came around the corner.
He saw me, and he said, what are you doing?
I said, well, I said, I'm a tailor.
So you're a tailor?
All day long, you guys aren't-- you know, laying around your bunks, not doing anything.
Why don't you take that day room building across the road and set up a tailor shop, until we call you to do something else?
So I'll excuse you from all further duties.
You're the company tailor from this point on.
I-- I wrote home to send me a sewing machine.
My parents sent me a sewing machine, and I was in business.
And for a year and a half, they took me off any duty roster.
I was no longer a gunnery instructor.
I no longer-- as far as the unit was concerned, people got promoted.
There was no promotion for a tailor.
That wasn't even called for.
So I was a buck private, and I was a tailor.
But he told me to charge nominal fees.
And a portion of that went to the company recreational fund, with which we bought a pool table, a ping pong table, the money that I made for the company.
And I had a wonderful life, until finally, the war broke out in Africa, and we invaded Africa.
I mean, the Germans had invaded, and we were-- and we landed our troops there.
And those wonderful days came to an end.
And they said, now, everyone that's physically A, fit, has to go into combat training and will be shipped overseas.
And that was the end of my tailoring career, at least for the time being.
The-- in-- in-- in the United States?
Officer's Candidate School.
At the headquarters company.
Oh, yes.
It was the headquarters company of the Officers Candidate School in-- in what was then Camp Hood.
Absolutely.
They shipped-- they shipped me to a tank destroyer replacement depo, it was called, in Camp Kilmer, New Jersey.
That was in March of 19-- in-- in April of 1944.
The week prior to that, I got a week's leave.
And I, earlier on, had become engaged to my wife.
And we decided that, before going overseas, we wanted to get married.
So I had one week at home.
And during that week of March, of 1944, we got married, on the 30th of March, 1944.
And then, a week later, I shipped out to Camp Kilmer, to the tank destroyer replacement depo.
Where was the-- where was the ceremony?
In a private home.
We had-- we had-- there was no money in those days to have fancy weddings.
It was a very simple, small wedding in a private home.
Oh, yes.
Rabbi Baumberger, who used to be a rabbi in Essen, in Germany-- that was very strange-- who married us.
He-- he-- Rabbi Baumberger was a rabbi here in Chicago.
And but strange as it seems, he was also, for a while, the rabbi of our congregation in Essen.
He knew my dad.
And he married us.
Yes.
He got-- he got out.
He got out to England, where he died, eventually.
He died of natural causes, in England.
Mm-hm.
One week.
No, it was less than a-- almost a week, maybe five or six days.
That's all.
Just a very short time.
Yes.
From there, a field hospital came through Camp Kilmer.
And it was-- the Army always saw to it that any unit that passed through the Camp Kilmer had to have enough manpower.
In other words, if the unit called for 300 soldiers, and there were 298, they would take two people out of the replacement depo and put them into the unit.
Field hospital was short one man with a classification-- I think it was called 24294, which called for a tailor.
They didn't have a tailor.
And as luck happened, I was the tailor who was in this replacement depo.
So they put me onto a train to go with this unit in the afternoon of the fifth or sixth day at Camp Kilmer.
And two hours later, I found myself aboard a huge troop transport in those days.
There must have been 10,000 of us aboard ship on the way to England.
And so you landed in England?
In England, near Glasgow.
We landed in April of 1944, in England.
We all knew that the purpose of this huge troop shipment to England was eventually to invade Europe.
We-- we knew that, because, at that time, we had already invaded North Africa.
We were fighting in-- in Italy, I believe.
And yes, absolutely, we knew what was going to happen to us.
There was no doubt in our minds.
We landed on Omaha Beach on the 30th of June, 1944.
Oh, yes.
Oh, absolutely.
Yeah.
Well, first-- well, before joining the field hospital, I had combat training.
Oh, yes, definitely real.
All combat training, from obstacle courses, to weapons training, you name it.
But then, when I entered-- when I was-- joined the 54th Army field hospital, weapons training ceased.

Even so, when, in the latter stages of the war, in particular around the time when we were ready to invade Germany, we were issued weapons, because they no longer respected the Red Cross, which we wore around our arm.
And we had many casualties where they bombed ambulances, as well as killed Red Cross-- medics with the Red Cross badges.
So we were issued weapons later on.
But all through France, we were just active, running a field hospital, both treating our wounded, as well as some German prisoners.
Absolutely.
We were always attached to a combat unit.
A field hospital is the front line hospital.
That's the first unit-- the moment somebody gets hit or wounded, that-- the moment that happens, they put them into a field hospital.
